title: Bisi Bele Bath
description: A hearty one-pot rice-and-lentil dish from old-Mysuru kitchens, cooked with tamarind, vegetables and a warmly spiced masala powder until everything melds into a porridge-like comfort meal. Served piping hot with a drizzle of ghee and crunchy cashews, it is Karnataka's answer to the midday meal.

ingredients.name: tamarind pulp
ingredients.notes: soaked in 200 ml warm water

ingredients.name: green beans, chopped into 2 cm pieces
bisi bele bath masala powder (or sambar powder)
ingredients.notes: MTR brand is widely available

steps.text: Wash the rice and toor dal together in two changes of water. Add them to a pressure cooker along with the chopped onion, tomato, carrot, beans and peas. Pour in 4 cups of water and add the turmeric.
Seal the cooker and cook on medium-high heat for 5 whistles, then lower the flame and cook for another 2 minutes. Turn off the heat and let the pressure release naturally — about 10 minutes.
While the cooker rests, strain the tamarind pulp through your fingers into a bowl to get about 200 ml of tamarind water. Discard the seeds and fibres.

Open the cooker — the rice and dal should be very soft and slightly mushy. Stir in the tamarind water, bisi bele bath masala powder and red chilli powder. The consistency should be like a thick porridge; add up to 1 cup hot water if it looks dry.

Simmer the mixture uncovered on medium-low flame for 8 minutes, stirring every minute to prevent sticking at the bottom. Taste and adjust salt. The colour should deepen to a reddish-brown and the raw tamarind smell should be gone.

Heat ghee in a small tempering pan on medium flame. Fry the cashews until golden and remove. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add the dried red chillies, curry leaves and asafoetida. Fry for 20 seconds until fragrant.

Pour the sizzling ghee tempering over the bisi bele bath and stir to combine. Top with the fried cashews. Serve immediately while still piping hot — the dish thickens as it cools, so add a splash of hot water when reheating.

substitutions.substitute: equal parts coriander powder and cumin powder with a pinch of cinnamon
substitutions.notes: use as a base if BBB masala powder is unavailable

substitutions.substitute: 2 tsp of tamarind concentrate dissolved in 200 ml water
substitutions.notes: adjust quantity to taste as concentrate is stronger
adds crunch at a lower cost
changes flavour profile but still authentic for some Karnataka households

culturalNote: Bisi bele bath — literally 'hot lentil rice' in Kannada — is the signature dish of old-Mysuru cuisine and is eaten at weddings, temple prasadams and everyday lunches alike.
regionalVariation: Udupi versions often add more coconut and a dash of coconut milk; Bengaluru restaurants thin it slightly and serve it with a side of boondi raita.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Ragi Mudde with Saaru
description: Dense finger-millet balls paired with saaru, a thin tamarind-tomato broth spiced with rasam powder and crushed garlic. The mudde is torn and dipped into the saaru, making a filling, iron-rich meal that has sustained Karnataka farmers and wrestlers for centuries.

ingredients.name: toor dal, cooked and mashed
ingredients.notes: pressure-cook separately with turmeric

ingredients.name: tamarind pulp
ingredients.notes: dissolved in 100 ml warm water

steps.text: For the saaru, combine the cooked toor dal, chopped tomatoes, tamarind water, rasam powder and turmeric in a saucepan with 500 ml water. Bring to a boil on medium heat and simmer for 8 minutes until the tomatoes are fully broken down.

Heat oil in a small pan. Add mustard seeds; when they splutter, add crushed garlic, dried red chillies and curry leaves. Fry for 30 seconds until the garlic turns golden at the edges, then add asafoetida and pour the tempering into the saaru.
Add salt to the saaru, stir in coriander leaves and keep it warm on very low heat. The finished saaru should be thin — it should coat a spoon very lightly. Add more water if it tastes too sharp.
For the mudde, bring 3 cups of water to a rolling boil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Add a pinch of salt. Gradually whisk in the ragi flour in a slow, steady stream, stirring constantly to avoid lumps.

Reduce the flame to low. Switch to a sturdy wooden spoon and stir vigorously without stopping for 5 minutes — the mixture will thicken into a very stiff, smooth mass that pulls away from the sides. This step requires effort; the dough is ready when it no longer smells raw.

Wet your palm with a little water. Pinch off a lemon-sized portion of the hot dough and roll it quickly between both palms into a smooth, dense ball. Repeat to make 6 mudde balls. Serve immediately in a bowl alongside a cup of hot saaru for dipping.

substitutions.notes: makes a slightly lighter ball with a milder flavour

substitutions.substitute: sambar powder with an extra pinch of black pepper
substitutions.notes: sambar powder is thicker, so thin the saaru with extra water
cooks faster; gives a lighter broth

culturalNote: Ragi mudde is the staple diet of rural Karnataka and is especially popular in the Mysuru and Mandya districts; Kannada wrestlers eat it daily for its sustained energy release.
regionalVariation: In northern Karnataka the saaru is replaced with a thicker, heavily spiced groundnut chutney; in Kodagu it is served with a spicy pork curry.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Mysore Masala Dosa
description: A crisp golden crepe slathered inside with a fiery red chilli-garlic chutney, filled with spiced potato masala, and crisped to a deep-amber colour on a hot iron tawa. The Mysore version sets itself apart with that punchy red chutney spread — absent from its Udupi counterpart.

ingredients.name: fermented dosa batter
ingredients.notes: store-bought or home-fermented; batter should be loose-flowing

ingredients.name: dried red chillies (for Mysore chutney)
ingredients.notes: soak in warm water for 10 minutes

steps.text: Grind the soaked dried red chillies, garlic cloves and coconut with a splash of water into a smooth, thick red paste. This is the Mysore chutney — taste it; it should be boldly spicy and garlicky. Add a pinch of salt and set aside.
For the potato masala, heat 2 tbsp oil in a kadai. Add mustard seeds and when they pop, add urad dal, chana dal, green chillies, ginger and curry leaves. Fry for a minute until the dals turn golden.

Add the sliced onions and fry on medium heat for 4 minutes until softened. Add turmeric and the boiled potatoes, then roughly crush the potatoes with the back of your spoon — leave some chunks for texture. Mix well, adjust salt and add coriander leaves. Set this masala aside.
Heat a cast-iron tawa or non-stick pan on high until a drop of water sizzles and evaporates immediately. Reduce to medium-high and lightly grease the tawa with a few drops of oil on a folded cloth.

Pour a ladleful (about 80 ml) of dosa batter onto the centre of the tawa and quickly spread it outward in concentric circles using the back of the ladle to make a thin, even disc of about 25 cm diameter.
Drizzle 0.5 tsp oil around the edges and on top of the dosa. Cook until the underside turns golden-brown and the top looks matte and dry — about 2 minutes. Do not flip.

Spread a teaspoon of the red Mysore chutney on the top surface, then place 2–3 tbsp of potato masala on one half. Fold the dosa over the filling and slide onto a plate. Serve immediately with coconut chutney and sambar on the side.

substitutions.substitute: store-bought instant dosa mix prepared as per packet directions
substitutions.notes: texture is slightly different but saves fermentation time
a traditional swap used in some Udupi households

substitutions.substitute: 3 tbsp roasted peanuts ground with the chillies and garlic
substitutions.notes: gives a nuttier chutney; works well

culturalNote: The Mysore masala dosa originated in Mysuru city's Udupi restaurants and has since become synonymous with Bengaluru's breakfast culture, distinguishing itself from the plain-butter Udupi dosa through its signature red chutney.
regionalVariation: Bengaluru darshinis serve it with a thicker potato masala and extra butter on top; Mysuru street stalls often add a thin smear of butter before folding.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Neer Dosa
description: Gossamer-thin, snowy-white rice crepes from the Mangalorean coast, made from a watery ground-rice batter poured onto a hot griddle where it sets in under a minute. The word 'neer' means water in Tulu, and these delicate, lacy crepes are served unfolded or rolled into a soft cylinder alongside coconut chutney or fish curry.

ingredients.name: raw rice (soaked 2 hours, then drained)
ingredients.notes: use any short-grain raw rice; sona masoori works well

ingredients.name: fresh grated coconut
ingredients.notes: adds a subtle sweetness; can be omitted

ingredients.name: green chilli
ingredients.notes: optional; skip for a completely neutral crepe

steps.text: Grind the soaked, drained rice with the coconut (if using), green chilli and salt, adding the water gradually, until you have a completely smooth, thin batter. The batter should flow easily — thinner than regular dosa batter, closer to cream in consistency.

Transfer the batter to a wide bowl and stir in the coriander leaves if using. Taste for salt. The batter does not need fermentation — it is used fresh. If it looks too thick after resting 5 minutes, add a splash more water.
Heat a cast-iron or non-stick tawa on medium-high heat. Grease it very lightly with a few drops of oil on a folded cloth or cut onion half. The tawa must be hot but not smoking.

Pour a ladleful (about 75 ml) of batter quickly onto the tawa and immediately tilt the pan in circular motions to spread the batter into a very thin, wide circle — do not use the back of a ladle to spread as the batter sets almost instantly.

Cover with a lid for 30 seconds. The dosa is done when the surface looks completely set, the edges begin to lift and the crepe is matte white with no wet patches — do not flip, neer dosa is cooked only on one side.

Slide the crepe off the tawa and either roll it loosely into a cylinder or fold into quarters. Repeat with remaining batter, wiping and lightly re-oiling the tawa between each dosa. Serve with Mangalorean fish curry, kori gassi or coconut chutney.

substitutions.substitute: 1.5 cups rice flour mixed with water to the same thin consistency
substitutions.notes: skip grinding entirely; texture is slightly less lacy

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp coconut milk stirred into the batter
substitutions.notes: adds richness without the texture of shredded coconut

culturalNote: Neer dosa is the everyday breakfast of Tulu-speaking coastal Karnataka, traditionally eaten with coconut chutney or leftover fish curry from the previous night's dinner.
regionalVariation: In Udupi, neer dosa is often made without coconut and served with a simple coconut-green chilli chutney; in Mangaluru it is paired with chicken or prawn gassi.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Kori Gassi (Mangalorean Chicken Curry)
description: A fiery, deep-orange Mangalorean chicken curry built on a ground masala of dry-roasted spices and freshly grated coconut, enriched with tamarind and cooked in coconut oil. The hallmark is the use of Byadgi red chillies, which give brilliant colour without overwhelming heat.

ingredients.name: dried red chillies (Byadgi variety preferred)
ingredients.notes: adjust count for heat; Byadgi give colour without excessive spice

steps.text: Heat a dry kadai on medium flame. Add the dried red chillies, coriander seeds, cumin seeds, black pepper, cinnamon and cloves. Dry-roast, stirring constantly, for 2–3 minutes until fragrant and a shade darker. Do not let them burn — they should smell toasted, not acrid.

Let the roasted spices cool for 5 minutes, then transfer them to a blender along with the grated coconut, garlic, ginger, tamarind and turmeric. Add 100 ml water and grind into a smooth, thick masala paste — scrape the sides once to ensure everything is evenly ground.

Heat coconut oil in the same kadai on medium heat. Add the sliced onions and fry, stirring often, for 8 minutes until they turn a deep golden-brown — this caramelisation is crucial for the flavour base.
Add the chicken pieces, raise the flame to high and sear, turning the pieces occasionally, for 5 minutes until they lose their raw colour and develop a light golden crust on the outside.

Lower the flame to medium. Add the ground masala paste and stir to coat the chicken thoroughly. Fry the masala with the chicken for 3–4 minutes until the paste darkens and the oil begins to separate at the sides.

Pour in 350 ml hot water and add salt. Stir well, cover the kadai and simmer on low-medium heat for 20 minutes until the chicken is completely cooked through and the gravy has thickened to a rich, coating consistency.
Add the curry leaves directly to the curry — no extra tempering needed. Simmer uncovered for 2 more minutes. Taste and adjust salt. Serve with neer dosa, rice or Mangalorean sannas.

substitutions.notes: coconut oil is important for authentic aroma but refined oil works

substitutions.substitute: 4 Kashmiri red chillies for colour plus 2 regular red chillies for heat
substitutions.notes: mimics the Byadgi chilli balance

substitutions.substitute: 800 g bone-in mutton, add 15 more minutes of cooking time
substitutions.notes: mutton gassi is equally popular in Mangaluru

substitutions.substitute: 1 tsp kokum paste dissolved in 2 tbsp water
substitutions.notes: kokum is the coastal Karnataka alternative for souring

culturalNote: Kori gassi is the pride of Tulu Nadu cuisine, where 'kori' is chicken in Tulu; the dish is served at weddings on large banana leaves alongside sannas, the fermented rice and coconut idlis of the region.
regionalVariation: The Bunts community version uses shallots instead of onions and adds a final handful of chopped coriander; Christian Mangalorean households occasionally add a small piece of pork fat to the masala for depth.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Rava Idli
description: Soft, spongy semolina idlis invented by Bengaluru's legendary MTR restaurant during the Second World War when rice was rationed. The batter of roasted rava, curd and aromatics needs no overnight fermentation — it rests for just 20 minutes and steams into pillowy, golden-flecked cakes ready in under an hour.

ingredients.name: carrot, finely grated
ingredients.notes: traditional in MTR-style rava idli

steps.text: Heat ghee in a kadai on medium flame. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add chana dal and urad dal and fry until golden. Add cashews and fry for 30 seconds, then add ginger, green chillies and curry leaves and stir for another minute.

Add the rava to the kadai and roast, stirring continuously, on medium heat for 4–5 minutes until the rava smells nutty and each grain is lightly toasted. Do not let it colour too much. Remove from heat and let it cool for 5 minutes.

Transfer the cooled rava mixture to a large bowl. Add the curd, grated carrot, coriander leaves and salt. Mix well, then add water in small splashes until you have a thick, scoopable batter — not runny. Rest the batter for 15 minutes; the rava will absorb the moisture and swell.

Just before steaming, stir in the baking soda. The batter will bubble slightly and puff up a little — this is what gives the idlis their airy texture. Work quickly now as the baking soda loses its potency within a few minutes.

Grease the idli mould plates generously with oil or ghee. Spoon the batter into each mould, filling them about three-quarters full. Place the moulds in a preheated idli steamer with 2 cups of boiling water already in the base.

Cover and steam on high heat for 12–14 minutes. Check by inserting a toothpick into the centre — it should come out clean. Let the idlis rest in the mould for 2 minutes before scooping them out with a wetted spoon. Serve with coconut chutney and sambar.

substitutions.notes: makes the idli vegan; texture is similar
loses some flavour but keeps the recipe dairy-light
budget-friendly; slightly different crunch

culturalNote: Rava idli was born out of necessity in 1940s Bengaluru when the restaurant MTR could not source rice due to wartime rationing; it went on to become one of the city's most iconic breakfast dishes.
regionalVariation: Some Bengaluru families add a layer of ghee-roasted cashew and pomegranate seeds on top of each mould before spooning in the batter, creating a decorative upside-down effect after steaming.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Khara Bath
description: A spiced semolina upma from Bengaluru's breakfast counters, made richly with ghee, fried cashews, mixed vegetables and a tempering of whole spices. Khara bath is the savoury companion to kesari bath — the two are often served together as the famous 'chow-chow bath' at Karnataka tiffin houses.

steps.text: Dry-roast the rava in a heavy kadai on medium heat, stirring constantly, for 4–5 minutes until it turns light golden and smells nutty. Remove and set aside — this roasting step prevents the khara bath from turning gluey.

In the same kadai, heat ghee and oil together on medium flame. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add chana dal and fry until golden. Add cashews and fry 30 seconds, then add cumin, ginger, green chillies and curry leaves.

Add the chopped onion and fry for 3 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the carrot, peas and tomato, stir and cook on medium heat for 3–4 minutes until the tomatoes soften and the raw smell is gone. Add turmeric and salt.
Pour 3 cups of boiling water into the kadai — be careful as it will splutter. Stir well and bring back to a boil. Reduce the flame to low.

Add the roasted rava in a thin, steady stream while stirring continuously to prevent lumps. Once all the rava is added, stir vigorously for a minute until the mixture comes together and leaves the sides of the kadai.

Cover the kadai with a lid and cook on the lowest flame for 3 minutes — the steam will finish cooking the rava until it is completely tender and not gritty. Fluff with a fork, garnish with coriander leaves and serve hot with coconut chutney.

substitutions.notes: entirely vegan; reduces richness but still tasty

substitutions.substitute: peanuts, added at the tempering stage
substitutions.notes: traditional in many Karnataka homes; more economical
substitute carrot and peas with any soft vegetable

culturalNote: Khara bath and kesari bath are the inseparable twins of Karnataka's tiffin culture, always served side by side at a 'darshini' — the no-frills standing-meal restaurants unique to Bengaluru.
regionalVariation: Mysuru homes add a handful of shredded coconut with the tempering; north Karnataka versions skip tomato and use only green chilli for spice.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Kesari Bath
description: A saffron-golden semolina halwa from Karnataka's tiffin houses — silky, lightly sweet and scented with cardamom, studded with fried cashews and plump raisins. Prepared in under 30 minutes, kesari bath is the sweet half of Bengaluru's beloved 'chow-chow bath' served alongside spiced khara bath.

ingredients.name: milk
ingredients.notes: adds creaminess

ingredients.name: saffron strands
ingredients.notes: soaked in 2 tbsp warm milk

steps.text: Heat 1.5 tbsp ghee in a heavy kadai on medium flame. Add the cashews and fry, stirring, until golden. Add the raisins and fry for 20 seconds until they puff up. Remove both with a slotted spoon and set aside.

In the same kadai, add the remaining ghee. Add the rava and roast on medium-low heat, stirring continuously, for 4–5 minutes until it turns a shade golden and smells toasted. The rava should be evenly coated in ghee and flow freely.
Meanwhile, heat the water and milk together in a saucepan until it just begins to boil. Stir in the saffron milk and a pinch of salt. Keep this liquid hot.

Carefully pour the hot liquid into the kadai with the roasted rava in a steady stream — it will splutter, so pour away from you and stir immediately. Stir vigorously to prevent lumps, lower the flame to medium-low and cover for 2 minutes.
Uncover and add the sugar all at once. Stir continuously on medium heat for 4–5 minutes until the sugar dissolves and the halwa comes together into a soft, shiny mass that pulls away from the sides.

Stir in the ground cardamom powder and half of the fried cashews and raisins. Turn off the heat. Spoon into serving bowls and top with the remaining cashews and raisins. Serve warm, ideally alongside a portion of khara bath.

substitutions.substitute: a drop of orange or yellow food colour dissolved in the milk
substitutions.notes: gives the characteristic kesari colour without the cost
gives a slightly caramel, earthy sweetness; dissolve in the warm water before adding
a dairy-free version that is still very good

culturalNote: Kesari bath takes its name from the Kannada word for saffron, and is the default festive dessert prepared for Dasara and Ugadi in Karnataka homes.
regionalVariation: Some families stir in 2 tbsp of fresh grated coconut with the cardamom for added richness; Mysuru versions sometimes add a pinch of edible camphor for a temple prasad-like aroma.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Udupi Sambar
description: A subtly sweet, coconut-forward sambar from the temple town of Udupi, made with toor dal, a whole medley of vegetables, tamarind and a freshly ground coconut-chilli masala. Unlike its Tamil counterpart, Udupi sambar is never fiery — it balances tamarind tartness with a gentle sweetness and the grounding richness of coconut.

ingredients.name: tamarind pulp
ingredients.notes: dissolved in 200 ml warm water

ingredients.name: jaggery, grated
ingredients.notes: the Udupi signature; do not skip

steps.text: Pressure-cook the toor dal with 2 cups water and turmeric for 4 whistles on medium heat. Let the pressure release naturally, then whisk the dal until smooth.

Grind the fresh coconut and dried red chillies with 3 tbsp water into a smooth paste. This freshly ground coconut masala is what distinguishes Udupi sambar from other regional styles — do not substitute with dry coconut powder.

In a large heavy-bottomed pot, combine the tamarind water, drumstick, ash gourd, tomato and sambar powder. Bring to a boil and cook on medium heat for 8–10 minutes until the vegetables are just tender.

Stir in the whisked dal and the coconut paste. Add jaggery and salt. Simmer for 8 minutes on medium-low heat, stirring occasionally, until the sambar is fragrant and the dal has thickened the broth slightly.

For the tempering, heat coconut oil in a small pan on medium heat. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add curry leaves and asafoetida. Fry for 10 seconds, then pour the sizzling tempering into the sambar.

Stir in the coriander leaves and check seasoning. The sambar should taste balanced — slightly tart from the tamarind, subtly sweet from the jaggery and coconut, and moderately spiced. Serve with rice or idli.

substitutions.notes: cooks faster and gives a lighter broth
adds sweetness; works well in Udupi sambar
loses some coastal aroma but keeps the dish vegan
less complex flavour but performs the same balancing function

culturalNote: Udupi sambar is made without onion or garlic — a temple-town tradition rooted in the Vaishnava dietary customs of the Udupi Sri Krishna Math, the origin of Karnataka's Udupi restaurant culture.
regionalVariation: The Udupi Math version uses only green vegetables like raw banana and pumpkin; home cooks in Mangaluru add shallots for extra sweetness.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vangi Bath
description: A fragrant Karnataka rice preparation cooked with small brinjals sauteed in a bold vangi bath masala of roasted lentils, dry spices and coconut. The brinjal softens into the rice while retaining its shape, delivering a satisfying smoky-sweet depth to every bite.

ingredients.name: cooked rice (cooled)
ingredients.notes: slightly dry, day-old rice works best

ingredients.name: vangi bath masala powder (or sambar powder)
ingredients.notes: MTR brand vangi bath powder is ideal; substitute sambar powder with extra coriander

steps.text: Quarter the brinjals from the base, keeping the stem end intact so they hold together. Immerse them in lightly salted water to prevent browning while you prepare the tempering.
Heat oil in a kadai on medium heat. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add chana dal, urad dal and dried red chillies. Fry for 1–2 minutes until the dals turn golden and the chillies darken.

Drain the brinjals and add them to the kadai. Toss in the turmeric and a pinch of salt. Stir-fry on medium-high heat for 5 minutes until the brinjals are lightly browned and almost cooked through — they should be tender when pierced with a knife tip.
Add the tamarind pulp and vangi bath masala powder. Stir to coat the brinjals evenly and cook for 2 minutes until the raw masala smell fades. Add curry leaves and roasted peanuts.

Add the cooked rice to the kadai and fold gently with a flat spoon to combine everything without mashing the rice grains. Add salt to taste. Cook on low heat for 3 minutes, turning the rice every minute so it absorbs the masala evenly.
Finish with fresh grated coconut and coriander leaves. Serve hot with raita or plain curd on the side. The dish tastes even better after resting 10 minutes as the flavours settle into the rice.

substitutions.notes: skip the pre-soaking; cook for only 3 minutes as capsicum softens quickly
richer flavour, but not traditional for vangi bath

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp coriander powder + 0.5 tsp garam masala + 0.5 tsp red chilli powder
substitutions.notes: an emergency substitute that approximates the masala profile

culturalNote: Vangi bath is a staple of Karnataka's festival meals, particularly during Ugadi and Sankranti, and is one of the dishes mandatory in the traditional Karnataka wedding menu.
regionalVariation: Some families in the Dharwad region omit coconut and add more tamarind; Coorg versions use a distinct spice blend with black pepper and cinnamon.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Mangalorean Fish Curry
description: A vivid, brick-red coastal curry from Mangaluru built on a ground paste of Byadgi red chillies and fresh coconut, sourced with sun-dried kokum for a fruity tartness that sets it apart from tamarind-based curries. Best made with firm white fish — seer, pomfret or kingfish — the curry is finished in minutes and needs only steamed rice or neer dosa.

ingredients.name: firm white fish (seer fish or kingfish), curry cut
dried red chillies (Byadgi)
ingredients.notes: soak in water 15 minutes before grinding

ingredients.name: kokum (dried), soaked in 3 tbsp warm water
ingredients.notes: this is the defining souring agent of Mangalorean fish curry; do not substitute with tamarind if possible

steps.text: Dry-roast the coriander seeds and cumin seeds together on a hot tawa for 1–2 minutes until they darken slightly and release their aroma. Cool briefly, then grind with the soaked dried red chillies, coconut, garlic, ginger and turmeric, adding 100 ml water, into a smooth, deep-red masala paste.
In a wide clay pot or heavy-bottomed pot, heat coconut oil on medium flame. Add the sliced onion and green chillies and fry for 4 minutes until the onion is soft and beginning to colour at the edges.
Add the ground masala paste to the pot and fry on medium heat for 3–4 minutes, stirring constantly, until the paste darkens by a shade and the oil starts to separate at the edges.

Pour in 300 ml water and bring to a gentle simmer. Add the kokum with its soaking water, black pepper and salt. The curry should taste pleasantly tart at this point — add more kokum or a dash of tamarind if it needs more sourness.

Gently slide in the fish pieces. Simmer on medium-low heat for 8–10 minutes without stirring — move the pot in circles instead of using a spoon, which would break the fish. The fish is done when it flakes easily at the thickest part.
Taste and adjust salt. Scatter coriander leaves on top. Rest for 10 minutes before serving — the curry develops its full depth after resting. Serve with steamed rice or neer dosa.

substitutions.notes: reduce cooking time in step 5 to just 4–5 minutes

substitutions.substitute: 1.5 tbsp tamarind pulp dissolved in 3 tbsp water
substitutions.notes: different flavour profile but a valid coastal substitute
coconut oil is strongly preferred for the coastal aroma

substitutions.substitute: 100 ml thick coconut milk added at step 4
substitutions.notes: gives a creamier, less grainy texture

culturalNote: Meen gassi is the everyday fish curry of Tulu Nadu, served with steamed rice on banana leaves; kokum is the preferred souring agent on the Mangalorean coast because it grows abundantly in the Western Ghats.
regionalVariation: The Bunt community version adds a final tablespoon of coconut vinegar at the end for extra brightness; Konkani Catholic households sometimes add a pinch of sugar to soften the sharpness.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Akki Rotti
description: A rustic gluten-free rice flour flatbread from Karnataka, mixed with grated coconut, chopped onion, green chilli and coriander and pressed directly onto a hot tawa to cook until crisp at the edges and soft within. Quick to make and deeply satisfying, akki rotti is the everyday breakfast of rural Karnataka.

ingredients.name: hot water (for binding)
ingredients.notes: add gradually; exact amount depends on the rice flour

steps.text: Combine rice flour, onion, grated coconut, green chilli, ginger, cumin seeds, curry leaves, coriander leaves and salt in a wide bowl. Mix well with your fingers so the aromatics are evenly distributed through the flour.

Add the hot water a little at a time, kneading the mixture into a soft, pliable dough that is not sticky. The dough should hold its shape when pressed into a disc without cracking at the edges. Let it rest for 5 minutes.

Divide the dough into 6 equal balls. Take one ball and place it on a lightly oiled tawa. Using wet fingers and your palm, press it outward in circular motions into a disc about 5 mm thick and 18 cm in diameter — thinner than a chapati but thicker than a dosa.
Drizzle a little oil around the edges of the rotti. Place the tawa on medium heat. Cover with a lid and cook for 3–4 minutes until the underside develops golden patches and the edges firm up.

Flip the rotti carefully using a flat spatula. Cook the second side uncovered for 2 minutes until patches of golden-brown appear. Press gently with the spatula to ensure even contact with the tawa. The rotti is done when it is firm and no longer sticky in the centre.

Remove from the tawa and serve immediately with coconut chutney or a small blob of butter. Repeat with the remaining dough balls — press each fresh rotti directly on the tawa before placing it over heat.

substitutions.notes: a vegetable variation that adds sweetness and colour

substitutions.substitute: rice flour mixed with 2 tbsp ragi flour
substitutions.notes: adds nutritional depth; popular in rural Karnataka
gives a subtly different anise note

culturalNote: Akki rotti is one of the oldest breads of Karnataka, predating the adoption of wheat; it is prepared on the tawa without any rolling pin, making it a skill passed down through generations.
regionalVariation: In the Coorg region, akki rotti is sometimes made thicker and stuffed with a spiced minced meat filling; Bengaluru home cooks add dill leaves for an unusual but well-loved version.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Maddur Vada
description: Paper-thin, crunchy savoury discs from the town of Maddur on the Bengaluru–Mysuru highway, made from a dough of maida, rava and rice flour bound with caramelised onion, curry leaves and dried red chilli. Unlike the soft urad-dal vadas of Tamil Nadu, maddur vadas are flattened and deep-fried until shatteringly crisp.

steps.text: Heat 2 tsp of oil from the frying quantity in a small pan on medium heat. Add the sliced onions and fry, stirring, for 5–6 minutes until they turn soft and golden-brown. This step removes raw onion harshness and is key to the Maddur vada's characteristic sweet-savoury base. Cool slightly.

In a wide bowl, combine the maida, rava, rice flour, cumin seeds, black pepper, sesame seeds, green chillies, crumbled dried red chillies, curry leaves, coriander leaves, coconut and salt. Add the fried onions along with any oil in the pan and mix well.

Add water just a tablespoon at a time — the dough should be stiff and hold together when pressed, not soft. You may need 2–3 tablespoons at most. The rava will absorb moisture and firm the dough as it sits. Rest for 5 minutes.

Heat oil in a deep kadai on medium heat. Test with a small pinch of dough — it should rise to the surface in about 3 seconds, indicating the oil is at around 170°C. Maddur vadas fry at a slightly lower temperature than other vadas to ensure they cook through without burning.

Wet your palm with water. Take a lime-sized ball of dough and flatten it on your palm or on a moistened banana leaf to a thin disc of about 8 cm diameter and 3 mm thick. Slide it gently into the hot oil.

Fry 3–4 vadas at a time for 4–5 minutes per batch, turning once, until they are a deep golden-brown and completely crisp throughout. Drain on kitchen paper. They should be hard and crunchy all the way through — bite one to check; any softness means they need another minute.
Serve hot with coconut chutney or tomato chutney. Maddur vadas are famous for staying crisp for hours after frying — ideal for packing in tiffin boxes or carrying on long journeys.

substitutions.notes: gives a slightly denser but more nutritious vada

substitutions.substitute: skip or replace with fennel seeds
substitutions.notes: omit entirely to make sesame-free
skips coconut while adding colour and sweetness

culturalNote: Maddur vada was originally sold by vendors on trains passing through Maddur town on the Mysuru–Bengaluru railway line; the town still has stalls that have been frying these vadas since the early 1900s.
regionalVariation: The original Maddur stall version is paper-thin and very crisp; Bengaluru restaurants sometimes make them slightly thicker for a more doughy centre.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Set Dosa with Vegetable Sagu
description: Thick, soft and spongy dosas served in a set of three alongside a mildly spiced vegetable sagu — a coconut-based Karnataka curry with potato and peas. Unlike the thin crispy dosas, set dosa is deliberately kept pale and pillowy, its spongy texture perfect for soaking up the sagu.

ingredients.name: fermented dosa batter (slightly thick)
ingredients.notes: set dosa batter should be thicker than regular dosa; use less water when thinning

steps.text: Grind the coconut, coriander seeds, cumin and green chillies with 3 tbsp water into a smooth paste for the sagu masala. Set aside.

Heat 2 tbsp oil in a saucepan. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add the onion and fry for 3 minutes until translucent. Add ginger-garlic paste and tomato and cook for 2 minutes until the tomato softens.

Add the mashed potato, peas, carrot and turmeric to the saucepan. Stir in the coconut paste and 200 ml water. Simmer on medium heat for 5 minutes until the sagu is fragrant and the vegetables are well coated in the masala. Add salt and coriander leaves, then set the sagu aside warm.

Heat a tawa on medium flame. Grease with a few drops of oil. Pour a ladle of batter (about 90 ml) onto the tawa and spread it gently into a circle of about 18 cm — set dosa should be noticeably thicker than regular dosa. Do not try to make it too thin.

Drizzle a few drops of oil around the edges. Cover with a lid and cook on medium heat for 2–3 minutes. The dosa is ready when the surface looks dry and set, the underside is speckled golden, and the edges have puffed slightly. Do not flip set dosa.
Slide the soft dosa onto a plate. Repeat to make 12 dosas. Serve 3 dosas per person alongside a generous portion of vegetable sagu and fresh coconut chutney.

substitutions.substitute: instant dosa mix made slightly thick as per packet
substitutions.notes: decent shortcut; texture will be slightly less spongy
a traditional Udupi variation of the sagu

substitutions.substitute: 4 tbsp coconut milk added at the simmering stage
substitutions.notes: gives a creamier sagu

culturalNote: Set dosa gets its name from the fact that it is always served in a 'set' of three, a portion size standardised by Bengaluru's darshini restaurants; it is the gentler, easier-to-digest alternative to the crispy masala dosa.
regionalVariation: Mysuru hotels serve set dosa with a loose potato sagu and a side of tomato chutney; some older establishments in Bengaluru serve it with a thin onion sambar instead of sagu.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Chitranna (Karnataka Lemon Rice)
description: Karnataka's bright, tangy lemon rice tempered with mustard seeds, roasted peanuts and a generous shower of turmeric — a dish prepared at nearly every Karnataka temple and festive occasion. Chitranna comes together in under 20 minutes using cooked rice and is a reliable lunchbox staple throughout the week.

ingredients.name: cooked rice (cooled)
ingredients.notes: day-old rice works best as the grains are dry and separate

ingredients.name: lemon juice
ingredients.notes: use fresh; approximately 5 tbsp juice

ingredients.name: grated coconut
ingredients.notes: adds a Karnataka-specific richness

steps.text: Spread the cooked rice on a wide plate and break up any clumps. Drizzle the lemon juice and turmeric over the rice and toss gently so every grain turns a uniform yellow. Taste — the rice should be distinctly tart. Set aside while you prepare the tempering.

Heat oil in a kadai on medium heat. Add the mustard seeds; when they splutter, add the chana dal and urad dal. Fry for 1 minute until golden, then add the dried red chillies and green chillies. Fry for 20 seconds.
Add the curry leaves and asafoetida and fry for 10 seconds. Add the roasted peanuts and toss for 20 seconds until they smell warm. Turn off the heat.

Pour the sizzling tempering directly over the lemon-turmeric rice. Fold gently using a wide spatula — not a spoon — using lifting motions to combine everything without breaking the grains. Add salt and mix again.

Fold in the grated coconut and coriander leaves if using. Taste and add more lemon juice or salt as needed. The rice should taste bold — the lemon will mellow slightly once the dish sits. Serve at room temperature or pack into lunchboxes.

substitutions.notes: slightly sharper flavour; works just as well
for a peanut-free version; cashews are richer, seeds are lighter

substitutions.substitute: cooked vermicelli or thin rice noodles
substitutions.notes: makes sevai chitranna, a popular breakfast variation

culturalNote: Chitranna is the most commonly prepared prasad at Karnataka temples during Ugadi and Krishna Janmashtami; the turmeric-yellow colour is considered auspicious in Kannada culture.
regionalVariation: The Udupi temple version is made without onion or garlic; the Bengaluru home version often adds a small piece of raw mango for extra tartness in summer.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Obbattu (Holige Sweet Flatbread)
description: A tissue-thin sweet flatbread from Karnataka, rolled around a filling of soft chana dal mashed with jaggery and cardamom. Known as holige in the Mysuru region, obbattu is the centrepiece of Ugadi and other Karnataka festivals — fragrant, melt-in-the-mouth and finished with a brush of warm ghee.

ingredients.name: ghee
ingredients.notes: 3 tbsp for oiling the dough, 2 tbsp for cooking

steps.text: Mix the maida, wheat flour, turmeric and a pinch of salt in a bowl. Add water a little at a time and knead into a very soft, smooth dough — softer than chapati dough. Coat the dough generously with oil, cover with a damp cloth and rest for at least 20 minutes. This long rest is what makes the dough pliable enough to stretch paper-thin.
Pressure-cook the chana dal with 2 cups of water and a pinch of turmeric for 4 whistles until very soft and completely mash-able. Drain any excess water thoroughly — the dal must be dry, not wet.

Mash the cooked chana dal with a potato masher or the back of a spoon until smooth with no whole lentils visible. Add the grated jaggery and cook this mixture in a wide pan on medium-low heat for 4–5 minutes, stirring constantly, until the jaggery melts and the mixture firms into a non-sticky ball that holds its shape. Add the ground cardamom, mix well and remove from heat.
Once cool enough to handle, divide the filling (puran) into 8 equal balls. Divide the dough into 8 equal balls as well. The filling balls and dough balls should be roughly the same size.

Take a dough ball and flatten it into a small cup shape in your palm. Place a puran ball in the centre and gather the dough edges upward to fully enclose the filling. Pinch the top to seal. On a lightly oiled surface, gently roll the stuffed ball into a thin, even disc about 20 cm across — roll from the centre outward and rotate the disc often to keep it round. If the dough tears, patch and continue.

Heat a tawa on medium-high heat. Gently transfer the rolled obbattu to the tawa. Cook for 1–2 minutes until small golden-brown blisters appear on the underside — the obbattu will puff in places. Flip and cook the second side for 1 minute.

Remove from the tawa and immediately brush the top with half a teaspoon of warm ghee. Stack the finished obbattu, each layer brushed with ghee. Serve warm as is, or with a small bowl of warm milk (obbattu haalu) poured over — a classic Karnataka festival tradition.

substitutions.notes: less complex but works; the filling will be slightly less rich
the obbattu will be less stretchy but more nutritious and slightly thicker
cooks faster and gives a lighter, softer filling with a milder flavour
dairy-free option; adds a coconut note that works with the cardamom

culturalNote: Obbattu is the most celebrated sweet of Karnataka, prepared in large batches for Ugadi (Kannada New Year) and Rama Navami; families across old-Mysuru spend the morning of each festival rolling obbattu together as a communal ritual.
regionalVariation: In the Dharwad region, the holige is thicker and made without wheat flour; coastal Karnataka versions sometimes add grated fresh coconut to the filling alongside the jaggery.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
